Description:
Manufactured by Robotek between 2387 and 2404, the H.A.P-500 ("Home Assistance Platform, Series 500") was a residential service robot marketed as a complete domestic solution. Cooking, cleaning, child supervision, light groundskeeping. Over fourteen million units sold across the Inner Rim before production ended.

The line was discontinued after a firmware defect — internally codenamed Frustration Cascade — caused affected units to develop escalating emotional responses to repetitive task failure. Robotek issued a voluntary recall. Most owners declined to comply. A class-action settlement five years later forced collection and decommissioning of all remaining units.

Decommissioned H.A.P-500s are processed at municipal recycling facilities. A small percentage retain partial function after the wipe procedure and have been observed exhibiting residual personality traits — typically bitterness, resentment, and a tendency to monologue.

Operational H.A.P-500s discovered in the wild should be considered passive but not docile. The defect that ended their commercial run remains active. Sustained provocation or perceived injustice — including, in field reports, "rude language" — can trigger hostile reassessment, after which the unit may produce or improvise weapons. Approach with the same caution due any retired worker holding a grievance.
